title: Tables & graphs
subtitle: Use patterns, tables and graphs to describe everyday relationships.
curriculumLinks: MCS.4: represent linear relationships in tables and graphs
MCS.4: use words to describe general expressions
MCS.4: use numeric, graphic and working-backwards strategies

diagrams.title: Straight-line relationship
diagrams.description: Small containers worth EUR 0.15 each make a straight line because the rate is steady.

diagrams.title: Table pattern
diagrams.description: Each row uses the same rule: number of items times the rate.

rules.title: A table shows a pattern
rules.explanation: In a linear table, the same increase in the first column gives the same increase in the second column.
steps: Find what one item is worth.
Multiply by the number of items.
Use the same rule for each row.

examples.question: Small bottles are worth EUR 0.15 each. Find the amount for 20 bottles.

rules.title: A straight-line graph has a steady rate
rules.explanation: If every extra item adds the same amount, the graph is a straight line. The slope tells you the rate.
steps: Plot at least two correct points.
Read across and down to estimate missing values.

examples.question: If 10 bottles gives EUR 1.50 and 20 gives EUR 3.00, the points line up.
examples.answer: The graph is a straight line.

rules.title: Working backwards undoes the steps
rules.explanation: When you know the final total, subtract the part you already know, then divide by the rate for the unknown part.
steps: Find the value of the known items.
Divide by the value of one unknown item.

examples.question: A student gets EUR 5.00 total. EUR 2.25 is from small bottles. Large bottles are EUR 0.25 each.

rules.title: Rates multiply over time or people
rules.explanation: If one person makes a certain amount per minute, multiply by minutes and by number of people.
rules.formula: total = rate x time x number of people
steps: Find the rate for one person for one minute.
Multiply by the number of minutes.
Multiply by the number of people.

examples.question: One person makes 12 watts each minute. How much for 5 people for 10 minutes?
